Grouping of folk music genres

**Celtic music** is a broad grouping of [music genres](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that evolved out of the [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") traditions of the [Celtic people](/wiki/Celts_(modern) "Celts (modern)") of [Northwestern Europe](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe "Northwestern Europe") (the modern [Celtic nations](/wiki/Celtic_nations "Celtic nations")).[[1]](#cite_note-melhuish-1)[[2]](#cite_note-alberro-2) It refers to both orally-transmitted traditional music and recorded music and the styles vary considerably to include everything from traditional music to [a wide range of hybrids](/wiki/Celtic_fusion "Celtic fusion").

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Celtic_music&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Welsh_Druid_playing_the_harp_02400.jpg/250px-Welsh_Druid_playing_the_harp_02400.jpg)](/wiki/File:Welsh_Druid_playing_the_harp_02400.jpg)

An 18th century depiction of an ancient Druid playing the [Welsh harp](/wiki/Triple_harp "Triple harp")

The term *Celtic music* denotes the music of the people that identify as [Celts](/wiki/Celts_(modern) "Celts (modern)") of the [Celtic nations](/wiki/Celtic_nations "Celtic nations"). Many notable Celtic musicians such as [Alan Stivell](/wiki/Alan_Stivell "Alan Stivell") and [Paddy Moloney](/wiki/Paddy_Moloney "Paddy Moloney")[[3]](#cite_note-melhuishChieftains-3) claim that the different Celtic music genres have a lot in common.[[1]](#cite_note-melhuish-1)[[2]](#cite_note-alberro-2)[[4]](#cite_note-aubrey-4)

These styles are known because of the importance of Irish and Scottish people in the English speaking world, especially in the United States, where they had a profound impact on [American music](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_States "Music of the United States"), particularly [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music") and [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The [music of Wales](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Music_of_Cornwall "Music of Cornwall"), the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Music_of_the_Isle_of_Man "Music of the Isle of Man"), [Brittany](/wiki/Music_of_Brittany "Music of Brittany"), [Galician traditional music](/wiki/Galician_traditional_music "Galician traditional music") (Spain) and [music of Portugal](/wiki/Music_of_Portugal "Music of Portugal") are also considered Celtic music. The tradition is particularly strong in [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) where Celtic festivals take place throughout the year,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and in Wales, where the ancient [eisteddfod](/wiki/Eisteddfod "Eisteddfod") tradition was revived and flourishes. Celtic music is prevalent abroad as well, especially [in Canada](/wiki/Celtic_music_in_Canada "Celtic music in Canada") and the [United States](/wiki/Celtic_music_in_the_United_States "Celtic music in the United States"). The provinces of [Atlantic Canada](/wiki/Atlantic_Canada "Atlantic Canada") are known for being a home of Celtic music, most notably on the islands of [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)"), [Cape Breton](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island") and [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"). The traditional music of Atlantic Canada is heavily influenced by the Irish, Scottish and [Acadian](/wiki/Acadians "Acadians") ethnic makeup of much of the region's communities.[[8]](#cite_note-melhuishcb-8) In some parts of Atlantic Canada, such as Newfoundland, Celtic music is as or more popular than in the old country.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Some older forms of Celtic music that are rare in Scotland and Ireland today, such as the practice of accompanying a fiddle with a piano, or the Gaelic spinning songs of Cape Breton, remain common in [the Maritimes](/wiki/The_Maritimes "The Maritimes"). Much of the music of this region is Celtic in nature, but originates in the local area and celebrates the sea, seafaring, fishing and other primary industries.

Instruments associated with Celtic Music include the [Celtic harp](/wiki/Celtic_harp "Celtic harp"), [uilleann pipes](/wiki/Uilleann_pipes "Uilleann pipes") or [Great Highland bagpipe](/wiki/Great_Highland_bagpipe "Great Highland bagpipe"), [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), [tin whistle](/wiki/Tin_whistle "Tin whistle"), [flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute"), [bodhrán](/wiki/Bodhr%C3%A1n "Bodhrán"), [bones](/wiki/Bones_(instrument) "Bones (instrument)"), [concertina](/wiki/Concertina "Concertina"), [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion") and a recent addition, the [Irish bouzouki](/wiki/Irish_bouzouki "Irish bouzouki").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The mandolin has become increasingly common in Scottish folk ensembles during the 21st century, particularly within acoustic duos and contemporary folk groups drawing influence from both traditional dance tunes and modern Celtic arrangements.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Celtic_music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Divisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/A_Stivell_072707_Nuremberg_02.JPG/250px-A_Stivell_072707_Nuremberg_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:A_Stivell_072707_Nuremberg_02.JPG)

Alan Stivell at Nuremberg, Germany, 2007

In *Celtic Music: A Complete Guide*, June Skinner Sawyers acknowledges six [Celtic nationalities](/wiki/Celtic_nations "Celtic nations") divided into two groups according to their linguistic heritage.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [Q-Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages#Classification "Celtic languages") nationalities are the Irish, Scottish and [Manx peoples](/wiki/Manx_people "Manx people"), while the [P-Celtic](/wiki/P-Celtic "P-Celtic") groups are the [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people"), [Bretons](/wiki/Breton_people "Breton people") and [Welsh peoples](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"). Musician [Alan Stivell](/wiki/Alan_Stivell "Alan Stivell") uses a similar dichotomy, between the [Gaelic](/wiki/Goidelic_languages "Goidelic languages") (Irish/Scottish/Manx) and the [Brythonic](/wiki/Brythonic_languages "Brythonic languages") (Breton/Welsh/Cornish) branches, which differentiate "mostly by the extended range (sometimes more than two octaves) of Irish and Scottish melodies and the closed range of Breton and Welsh melodies (often reduced to a half-octave), and by the frequent use of the pure [pentatonic scale](/wiki/Pentatonic_scale "Pentatonic scale") in Gaelic music."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

There is also tremendous variation between *Celtic* regions. Ireland, Scotland, Wales, Cornwall, and Brittany have living traditions of language and music, and there has been a recent major revival of interest in Celtic heritage in the Isle of Man. [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)") has a Celtic language revival movement to revive the Q-Celtic *[Gallaic language](/wiki/Gallaecian_language "Gallaecian language")* used into Roman times,[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) which is not an [attested language](/wiki/Attested_language "Attested language"),[[17]](#cite_note-CDCG-17) unlike [Celtiberian](/wiki/Celtiberian_language "Celtiberian language"). A [Brythonic language](/wiki/Britonia "Britonia") may have been spoken in parts of Galicia and [Asturias](/wiki/Asturias "Asturias") into early Medieval times brought by Britons fleeing the [Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") via [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany").,[[18]](#cite_note-bretonmigrations-18)[[19]](#cite_note-thecelts-19) but here again there are several hypotheses and very little traces of it : lack of archeological, linguistic evidence and documents.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language") currently spoken in Galicia, [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") (*Galego*) is closely related to the [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") used mainly in Brazil and Portugal and in many ways closer to Latin than other Romance languages.[[17]](#cite_note-CDCG-17) Galician music is claimed to be *Celtic*.[[2]](#cite_note-alberro-2) The same is true of the music of Asturias,[[2]](#cite_note-alberro-2) [Cantabria](/wiki/Cantabria "Cantabria"), and that of Northern [Portugal](/wiki/Music_of_Portugal "Music of Portugal") (some say even traditional music from Central Portugal can be labeled Celtic).

[Breton](/wiki/Breton_people "Breton people") artist [Alan Stivell](/wiki/Alan_Stivell "Alan Stivell") was one of the earliest musicians to use the word *Celtic* and *Keltia* in his marketing materials, starting in the early 1960s as part of the worldwide folk music revival of that era[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-22) with the term quickly catching on with other artists worldwide. Today, the genre is well established and [incredibly diverse](/wiki/Celtic_fusion "Celtic fusion").

## Forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Celtic_music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Forms")]

There are musical genres and styles specific to each Celtic country, due in part to the influence of individual song traditions and the characteristics of specific languages:[[23]](#cite_note-23)

* [Celtic traditional music](/wiki/Folk_music#Celtic_traditional_music "Folk music")
* [Music of Ireland](/wiki/Music_of_Ireland "Music of Ireland")
* [Music of Scotland](/wiki/Music_of_Scotland "Music of Scotland")
* [Music of Wales](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales")
* [Strathspeys](/wiki/Strathspey_(dance) "Strathspey (dance)") are specific to Highland Scotland and Cape Breton, for example, and it has been hypothesized that they mimic the rhythms of the [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") language.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* [Reels](/wiki/Reel_(dance) "Reel (dance)")
* [Pibroch](/wiki/Pibroch "Pibroch")
* [Cerdd Dant](/wiki/Cerdd_Dant "Cerdd Dant") (string music) or *Canu Penillion* (verse singing) is the art of vocal improvisation over a given [melody](/wiki/Melody "Melody") in Welsh musical tradition. It is an important competition in [eisteddfodau](/wiki/Eisteddfod "Eisteddfod"). The singer or (small) [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir") sings a counter melody over a [harp](/wiki/Harp "Harp") melody.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* [Waulking song](/wiki/Waulking_song "Waulking song")
* [Puirt à beul](/wiki/Puirt_%C3%A0_beul "Puirt à beul")
* [Kan ha diskan](/wiki/Kan_ha_diskan "Kan ha diskan")
* [Sean-nós singing](/wiki/Sean-n%C3%B3s_singing "Sean-nós singing")
* [Celtic hip-hop](/wiki/Celtic_hip-hop "Celtic hip-hop")
* [Celtic rock](/wiki/Celtic_rock "Celtic rock")
* [Celtic metal](/wiki/Celtic_metal "Celtic metal")
* [Celtic punk](/wiki/Celtic_punk "Celtic punk")
* [Celtic fusion](/wiki/Celtic_fusion "Celtic fusion")
* [Progressive music](/wiki/Progressive_music "Progressive music")
* [Folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music")

## Celtic fusion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Celtic_music&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Celtic fusion")]

Main article: [Celtic fusion](/wiki/Celtic_fusion "Celtic fusion")

The oldest musical tradition which fits under the label of Celtic fusion originated in the rural American south in the early colonial period and incorporated English, Scottish, Irish, Welsh, German, and African influences. Variously referred to as roots music, American folk music, or [old-time music](/wiki/Old-time_music "Old-time music"), this tradition has exerted a strong influence on all forms of American music, including country, blues, and rock and roll.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In addition to its lasting effects on other genres, it marked the first modern large-scale mixing of musical traditions from multiple ethnic and religious communities within the [Celtic diaspora](/wiki/Celtic_diaspora_(disambiguation) "Celtic diaspora (disambiguation)").

In the 1960s several bands put forward modern adaptations of Celtic music pulling influences from several of the Celtic nations at once to create a modern [pan-celtic](/wiki/Pan-celtic "Pan-celtic") sound. A few of those include [bagado](/wiki/Bagad "Bagad")ù (Breton pipe bands), [Fairport Convention](/wiki/Fairport_Convention "Fairport Convention"), [Pentangle](/wiki/Pentangle_(band) "Pentangle (band)"), [Steeleye Span](/wiki/Steeleye_Span "Steeleye Span") and [Horslips](/wiki/Horslips "Horslips").

In the 1970s [Clannad](/wiki/Clannad "Clannad")[[34]](#cite_note-34) made their mark initially in the [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and traditional scene, and then subsequently went on to bridge the gap between traditional Celtic and pop music in the 1980s and 1990s, incorporating elements from [new-age](/wiki/New-age_music "New-age music"), [smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz"), and [folk rock](/wiki/Folk_rock "Folk rock"). Traces of Clannad's legacy can be heard in the music of many artists, including [Altan](/wiki/Altan_(band) "Altan (band)"), [Anúna](/wiki/An%C3%BAna "Anúna"), [Capercaillie](/wiki/Capercaillie_(band) "Capercaillie (band)"), [the Corrs](/wiki/The_Corrs "The Corrs"), [Dexys Midnight Runners](/wiki/Dexys_Midnight_Runners "Dexys Midnight Runners"), [Enya](/wiki/Enya "Enya"), [Loreena McKennitt](/wiki/Loreena_McKennitt "Loreena McKennitt"), [Riverdance](/wiki/Riverdance "Riverdance"), [Donna Taggart](/wiki/Donna_Taggart "Donna Taggart"), and [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2"). The solo music of Clannad's lead singer, [Moya Brennan](/wiki/Moya_Brennan "Moya Brennan") (often referred to as the First Lady of Celtic Music) has further enhanced this influence.

Later, beginning in 1982 with [the Pogues](/wiki/The_Pogues "The Pogues")' invention of Celtic folk-punk and [Stockton's Wing](/wiki/Stockton%27s_Wing "Stockton's Wing") blend of Irish traditional and Pop, Rock and Reggae, there has been a movement to incorporate [Celtic](/wiki/Celt "Celt") influences into other genres of music. Bands like [Flogging Molly](/wiki/Flogging_Molly "Flogging Molly"), [Black 47](/wiki/Black_47_(band) "Black 47 (band)"), [Dropkick Murphys](/wiki/Dropkick_Murphys "Dropkick Murphys"), the Young Dubliners, [the Tossers](/wiki/The_Tossers "The Tossers") introduced a hybrid of [Celtic rock](/wiki/Celtic_rock "Celtic rock"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [hardcore](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk") and other elements in the 1990s that has become popular with Irish-American youth.

Today there are Celtic-influenced subgenres of virtually every type of popular music including [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), [rock](/wiki/Celtic_rock "Celtic rock"), [metal](/wiki/Celtic_metal "Celtic metal"), [punk](/wiki/Celtic_punk "Celtic punk"), [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [new-age](/wiki/New-age_music "New-age music"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin_music_(genre) "Latin music (genre)"), [Andean](/wiki/Andean_music "Andean music") and [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music"). Collectively these modern interpretations of Celtic music are sometimes referred to as [Celtic fusion](/wiki/Celtic_fusion "Celtic fusion").

## Other modern adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Celtic_music&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other modern adaptations")]

Outside of America, the first deliberate attempts to create a "Pan-Celtic music" were made by the Breton [Taldir Jaffrennou](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Jaffrennou "François Jaffrennou"), having translated songs from Ireland, Scotland, and Wales into Breton between the two world wars. One of his major works was to bring "[Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")" (the Welsh [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem")) back in Brittany and create lyrics in Breton. Eventually this song became "*Bro goz va zadoù*" ("Old land of my fathers") and is the most widely accepted Breton anthem. In the 70s, the Breton [Alan Cochevelou](/wiki/Alan_Cochevelou "Alan Cochevelou") (future [Alan Stivell](/wiki/Alan_Stivell "Alan Stivell")) began playing a mixed repertoire from the main Celtic countries on the Celtic harp his father created.[[22]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-22)   
Probably the most successful all-inclusive Celtic music composition in recent years is [Shaun Daveys](/wiki/Shaun_Davey "Shaun Davey") composition [*The Pilgrim*](/wiki/The_Pilgrim_(1983_album) "The Pilgrim (1983 album)"). This suite depicts the journey of St. Colum Cille through the Celtic nations of Ireland, Scotland, the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), Wales, [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany") and [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)"). The suite which includes a Scottish pipe band, Irish and Welsh harpists, [Galician gaitas](/wiki/Galician_gaita "Galician gaita"), Irish [uilleann pipes](/wiki/Uilleann_pipes "Uilleann pipes"), the [bombardes](/wiki/Bombard_(music) "Bombard (music)") of [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"), two vocal soloists and a narrator is set against a background of a classical orchestra and a large choir.

Modern music may also be termed "Celtic" because it is written and recorded in a Celtic language, regardless of musical style. Many of the Celtic languages have experienced resurgences in modern years, spurred on partly by the action of artists and musicians who have embraced them as hallmarks of identity and distinctness. In 1971, the Irish band *[Skara Brae](/wiki/Skara_Brae_(band) "Skara Brae (band)")* recorded its only [LP](/wiki/LP_album "LP album") (simply called *Skara Brae*), all songs in Irish. In 1978 [Runrig](/wiki/Runrig "Runrig") recorded an album in [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"). In 1992 [Capercaillie](/wiki/Capercaillie_(band) "Capercaillie (band)") recorded "A Prince Among Islands", the first Scottish Gaelic language record to reach the UK top 40. In 1996, a song in Breton represented France in the [41st Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1996 "Eurovision Song Contest 1996"), the first time in history that France had a song without a word in French. Since about 2005, [Oi Polloi](/wiki/Oi_Polloi "Oi Polloi") (from Scotland) have recorded in Scottish Gaelic. [Mill a h-Uile Rud](/wiki/Mill_a_h-Uile_Rud "Mill a h-Uile Rud") (a [Scottish Gaelic punk](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_punk "Scottish Gaelic punk") band from Seattle) recorded in the language in 2004.

Several contemporary bands have Welsh language songs, such as [Ceredwen](/wiki/Ceredwen "Ceredwen"), which fuses traditional instruments with [trip hop](/wiki/Trip_hop "Trip hop") beats, the [Super Furry Animals](/wiki/Super_Furry_Animals "Super Furry Animals"), [Fernhill](/wiki/Fernhill_(band) "Fernhill (band)"), and so on (see the [Music of Wales](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales") article for more Welsh and Welsh-language bands). The same phenomenon occurs in Brittany, where many singers record songs in Breton, traditional or modern (hip-hop, [rap](/wiki/Rap_music "Rap music"), and so on.).
